explanations.5: The `$#` variable counts how many arguments were passed to the script; this line checks that exactly one argument was provided, otherwise it shows usage and exits.
explanations.12: This checks if the folder doesn't exist (the `!` means 'not'), and if true, it prints an error and exits—the `&&` chains these three commands to run only if the previous one succeeds.
explanations.18: The `pushd` command changes into that folder and saves the current location on a stack so you can return to it later with `popd`; the `>/dev/null` hides the usual output.
explanations.20: Setting `set +e` tells bash to keep running even if a command fails, which is useful here because `diff` exits with a non-zero status when files differ.
explanations.23: The `diff --side-by-side` compares two lists and displays them in columns; the `<(...)` syntax runs a command and treats its output as a temporary file that `diff` can read.
explanations.27: After the diff command runs, `$?` holds its exit code (0 means identical, non-zero means different), so this checks if they weren't equal.
explanations.33: Setting `set -e` restores normal bash behavior so the script will exit immediately if any command fails after this point.
